questions.prompt: In Federalist No. 10, James Madison argues that the most effective safeguard against the dangers of faction in a large republic is which of the following?
choices: A strong, unified national religion that promotes shared values
The extended sphere of a large republic, which makes any single faction unlikely to form a tyrannical majority
Restricting voting rights to those who own substantial property in multiple states
Concentrating legislative power in a single chamber elected by direct popular vote
questions.correctText: The extended sphere of a large republic, which makes any single faction unlikely to form a tyrannical majority
questions.explanation: Madison's central claim in Federalist No. 10 is that a large republic dilutes factional power: the greater the variety of interests, the less likely any single faction can dominate. Pluralism, not suppression, contains the mischief of faction.

questions.prompt: Brutus No. 1 expresses concern about the proposed Constitution primarily because it argues that
choices: the Necessary and Proper Clause and Supremacy Clause would allow the national government to absorb state powers
a strong executive was indispensable to defending against foreign invasion
the document failed to create a federal judiciary capable of reviewing state laws
amendment procedures would make it too easy for transient majorities to alter the framework
questions.correctText: the Necessary and Proper Clause and Supremacy Clause would allow the national government to absorb state powers
questions.explanation: Brutus 1 is the canonical Anti-Federalist warning that a consolidated republic cannot remain free over such a vast territory and that the elastic clauses would let federal power swallow the states.

questions.prompt: Which weakness of the Articles of Confederation was most directly addressed by a specific provision of the 1787 Constitution?
choices: Congress lacked the power to tax, which was remedied by giving Congress authority to lay and collect taxes
Congress could not declare war, which was remedied by transferring that power to the president
States issued their own currency, which was remedied by abolishing all state governments
There was no Bill of Rights, which was remedied in the original text of the 1787 Constitution
questions.correctText: Congress lacked the power to tax, which was remedied by giving Congress authority to lay and collect taxes
questions.explanation: Under the Articles, Congress had to requisition funds from the states. Article I, Section 8 of the Constitution explicitly grants Congress the power to lay and collect taxes, fixing the single most crippling weakness of the Articles.

questions.prompt: The Great (Connecticut) Compromise resolved a dispute at the Constitutional Convention by
choices: counting enslaved persons as three-fifths of a person for representation and taxation
creating a bicameral legislature with representation by population in the House and equal state representation in the Senate
guaranteeing that the importation of enslaved persons could not be banned for twenty years
establishing the Electoral College as the method for selecting the president
questions.correctText: creating a bicameral legislature with representation by population in the House and equal state representation in the Senate
questions.explanation: The Great Compromise blended the Virginia Plan's proportional representation with the New Jersey Plan's equal-state representation by splitting them across two chambers.

questions.prompt: Which scenario best illustrates the constitutional principle of federalism?
choices: The Supreme Court strikes down a federal law as unconstitutional
The president vetoes legislation passed by Congress and Congress overrides the veto
A state legalizes recreational marijuana even though federal law continues to classify it as a controlled substance
The Senate confirms a presidential nominee to a cabinet position
questions.correctText: A state legalizes recreational marijuana even though federal law continues to classify it as a controlled substance
questions.explanation: Federalism is the division of authority between the national and state governments. Conflicting state and federal policy in the same regulatory area is a classic display of the federal structure in action.

questions.prompt: In McCulloch v. Maryland (1819), the Supreme Court held that
choices: states may tax federal institutions operating within their borders so long as the tax is uniform
Congress had implied power to charter a national bank and states could not tax that bank
the Tenth Amendment reserves to the states the power to regulate banking
the Bill of Rights applies to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment
questions.correctText: Congress had implied power to charter a national bank and states could not tax that bank
questions.explanation: Chief Justice Marshall read the Necessary and Proper Clause broadly to find an implied power to charter the Bank, and held that under the Supremacy Clause Maryland could not tax a federal instrumentality.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements from the Declaration of Independence best reflects the Enlightenment idea of social contract theory?
choices: Governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed
All laws repugnant to the Constitution shall be deemed null and void
The accumulation of all powers in the same hands is the very definition of tyranny
The Congress shall have power to lay and collect taxes
questions.correctText: Governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed
questions.explanation: Social contract theory holds that legitimate government rests on the consent of those it governs — Locke's premise, which Jefferson incorporated directly into the Declaration.

questions.prompt: The participatory, pluralist, and elitist models of democracy differ primarily in their views about
choices: whether the Constitution should be amended through Article V or by judicial interpretation
the proper degree and form of citizen involvement in governmental decision making
the appropriate balance of power between Congress and the president
whether the United States should remain a federal or unitary system
questions.correctText: the proper degree and form of citizen involvement in governmental decision making
questions.explanation: The three models compete over how much and through what channels ordinary citizens shape policy: broad direct participation, competition among organized interest groups, or rule by a small group of elites.

questions.prompt: A federal grant that comes with detailed conditions specifying how the funds must be used, such as setting a minimum drinking age in order to receive highway funds, is best classified as
choices: a block grant that gives states broad discretion within a policy area
a categorical grant that uses fiscal federalism to influence state policy
an unfunded mandate prohibited under contemporary federalism doctrine
a revenue-sharing transfer with no conditions attached
questions.correctText: a categorical grant that uses fiscal federalism to influence state policy
questions.explanation: Categorical grants come with narrow strings attached and let Congress use its spending power to nudge states toward national policy goals — a defining tool of fiscal federalism.

questions.prompt: The constitutional purpose of the Senate filibuster, although not mentioned in the Constitution, is best understood as a procedural practice that
choices: allows a simple majority of senators to bring debate to an immediate close
extends debate and effectively requires a three-fifths supermajority (60 votes) to invoke cloture on most legislation
requires the vice president to break tie votes on all procedural motions
limits each senator to one hour of debate on any contested bill
questions.correctText: extends debate and effectively requires a three-fifths supermajority (60 votes) to invoke cloture on most legislation
questions.explanation: Senate Rule XXII permits unlimited debate unless cloture is invoked by 60 senators, giving the minority leverage that the House — where the Rules Committee controls debate — lacks.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the most accurate comparison of the House of Representatives and the Senate?
choices: The House has the sole power to ratify treaties; the Senate has the sole power to originate revenue bills
The House sets terms of two years and originates revenue bills; the Senate sets terms of six years and confirms presidential appointments
Both chambers must concur in any presidential appointment to the Supreme Court
The House holds impeachment trials; the Senate brings articles of impeachment
questions.correctText: The House sets terms of two years and originates revenue bills; the Senate sets terms of six years and confirms presidential appointments
questions.explanation: These are the textbook structural contrasts: the House is the people's chamber with short terms and the origination clause; the Senate is the deliberative chamber with longer terms and the advice-and-consent role.

questions.prompt: Federalist No. 70 argues for which feature of the executive branch?
choices: A plural executive made up of three co-equal officers
An energetic, unitary executive capable of decisive action and clear accountability
A presidency limited to a single four-year term to prevent abuses of power
A presidency elected directly by the people rather than by electors
questions.correctText: An energetic, unitary executive capable of decisive action and clear accountability
questions.explanation: Hamilton in Federalist 70 maintains that 'energy in the executive' — unity, duration, adequate provision for support, and competent powers — is essential to good government, and that a single executive is more accountable than a board.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best illustrates the principle of checks and balances?
choices: The Speaker of the House sets the legislative agenda for the chamber
Congress overrides a presidential veto by a two-thirds vote in both chambers
The president holds weekly cabinet meetings to coordinate executive policy
A federal agency issues a rule interpreting a statute
questions.correctText: Congress overrides a presidential veto by a two-thirds vote in both chambers
questions.explanation: A veto override is a paradigmatic check: one branch (Congress) acts to limit another (the president) using a constitutionally specified procedure.

questions.prompt: Federalist No. 78 argues that the judiciary will be the least dangerous branch primarily because it
choices: is composed of elected officials directly accountable to the people
holds neither the power of the sword nor the purse and depends on the other branches to enforce its judgments
is constitutionally required to follow majority public opinion in its decisions
may be removed at any time by a simple majority vote of Congress
questions.correctText: holds neither the power of the sword nor the purse and depends on the other branches to enforce its judgments
questions.explanation: Hamilton famously calls the judiciary the 'least dangerous' branch because it cannot enforce its own rulings — it must rely on the executive — and so poses the smallest threat to liberty.

questions.prompt: Marbury v. Madison (1803) is foundational primarily because the Supreme Court
choices: ordered President Jefferson to deliver William Marbury's judicial commission
established the principle of judicial review by holding part of a federal statute unconstitutional
ruled that state courts could not hear cases involving federal officials
expanded the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court beyond what Article III provides
questions.correctText: established the principle of judicial review by holding part of a federal statute unconstitutional
questions.explanation: Chief Justice Marshall held Section 13 of the Judiciary Act of 1789 unconstitutional, establishing that 'it is emphatically the duty of the judicial department to say what the law is.' This is the origin of judicial review.

questions.prompt: The growth of the federal bureaucracy since the New Deal has most directly led to
choices: an increase in the number of constitutional amendments ratified each decade
the delegation of significant rule-making authority to agencies, which then issue regulations with the force of law
the abolition of state agencies in fields regulated by the federal government
the elimination of congressional oversight of the executive branch
questions.correctText: the delegation of significant rule-making authority to agencies, which then issue regulations with the force of law
questions.explanation: Congress frequently writes broad statutes and delegates the details to expert agencies, which then promulgate binding regulations. Rule making is now a primary site of policy creation.

questions.prompt: A discharge petition in the House of Representatives is best described as a procedure that
choices: allows the Speaker to remove a committee chair who has lost majority support
permits a majority of members (218) to force a bill out of committee and onto the floor
ends debate on a bill in the House Rules Committee
permits the Senate to take up a House-passed bill without referring it to committee
questions.correctText: permits a majority of members (218) to force a bill out of committee and onto the floor
questions.explanation: When a House committee bottles up a bill, 218 members may sign a discharge petition to bring it directly to the floor — a rare but powerful check on committee gatekeeping.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of the president's use of informal powers rather than a power explicitly granted in Article II?
choices: Negotiating and signing a treaty subject to Senate ratification
Issuing an executive agreement with a foreign leader that does not require Senate approval
Granting a pardon for a federal offense
Nominating a federal district court judge
questions.correctText: Issuing an executive agreement with a foreign leader that does not require Senate approval
questions.explanation: Executive agreements are not mentioned in the Constitution but have become a standard informal tool that lets presidents reach international understandings without the two-thirds Senate vote treaties require.

questions.prompt: In United States v. Lopez (1995), the Supreme Court held that
choices: Congress could regulate guns near schools because gun violence substantially affects interstate commerce
the federal Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's authority under the Commerce Clause
states could not enact gun regulations stricter than federal law
the Second Amendment is incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment
questions.correctText: the federal Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's authority under the Commerce Clause
questions.explanation: Lopez was the first time in roughly sixty years the Court struck down a federal law as exceeding the commerce power, holding that possessing a gun near a school was not economic activity substantially affecting interstate commerce.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the constitutional process for impeachment and removal of a federal officer?
choices: The Senate impeaches by majority vote; the House tries the case and removes by two-thirds vote
The House impeaches by majority vote; the Senate tries the case and removes by two-thirds vote
Either chamber may impeach by two-thirds vote and the Supreme Court tries the case
The Supreme Court impeaches by majority vote and Congress removes by two-thirds vote
questions.correctText: The House impeaches by majority vote; the Senate tries the case and removes by two-thirds vote
questions.explanation: Article I gives the House the 'sole Power of Impeachment' (a charging function, by simple majority) and the Senate the 'sole Power to try all Impeachments' (conviction requires two-thirds).

questions.prompt: Which scenario best demonstrates judicial restraint rather than judicial activism?
choices: A federal court strikes down a long-standing state law as inconsistent with a new reading of the Equal Protection Clause
A federal court defers to the legislature's judgment and upholds a contested statute under a rational basis standard
A federal court reads a constitutional provision more broadly than its text appears to require
A federal court orders a state to redraw its legislative districts to remedy malapportionment
questions.correctText: A federal court defers to the legislature's judgment and upholds a contested statute under a rational basis standard
questions.explanation: Restraint refers to courts deferring to the elected branches absent clear constitutional conflict; activism describes courts more willing to strike down or reinterpret laws.

questions.prompt: Engel v. Vitale (1962) held that
choices: a moment of silence during the school day violates the Establishment Clause
a state-composed nondenominational prayer recited in public schools violates the Establishment Clause
students may not be required to recite the Pledge of Allegiance
religious student groups may be barred from meeting on public school grounds after hours
questions.correctText: a state-composed nondenominational prayer recited in public schools violates the Establishment Clause
questions.explanation: Engel held that an official prayer authored by New York's Board of Regents and recited in public school was unconstitutional regardless of how brief or voluntary it was; government may not compose official prayers.

questions.prompt: Tinker v. Des Moines (1969) is most often cited for the principle that
choices: students do not shed their constitutional rights to freedom of speech at the schoolhouse gate
schools may impose any restriction on student speech as long as it is content-neutral
public schools may search students without any individualized suspicion
the Establishment Clause prohibits student-led prayer at public school events
questions.correctText: students do not shed their constitutional rights to freedom of speech at the schoolhouse gate
questions.explanation: The Court protected students wearing black armbands as symbolic anti-war speech, holding that schools may restrict student expression only when it would materially and substantially disrupt school operations.

questions.prompt: Schenck v. United States (1919) articulated which standard for limiting freedom of speech?
choices: Speech may be punished if it presents a clear and present danger of bringing about substantive evils Congress may prevent
Speech may be punished only if it constitutes a true threat directed at a specific individual
Speech may be punished only when it is directed to inciting imminent lawless action and is likely to do so
Speech may be punished whenever a majority of legislators find it offensive
questions.correctText: Speech may be punished if it presents a clear and present danger of bringing about substantive evils Congress may prevent
questions.explanation: Justice Holmes's Schenck opinion introduced the clear-and-present-danger test in the context of wartime opposition to the draft. (The standard was later narrowed by Brandenburg's imminent-lawless-action test.)

questions.prompt: New York Times Co. v. United States (1971), the Pentagon Papers case, is best understood as
choices: extending the Establishment Clause to cover state-funded newspapers
holding that the government carries a heavy presumption against the constitutional validity of prior restraints on the press
permitting the government to prosecute journalists who publish classified information
establishing that members of Congress may not be sued for statements made on the floor
questions.correctText: holding that the government carries a heavy presumption against the constitutional validity of prior restraints on the press
questions.explanation: The Court refused the Nixon administration's request to enjoin publication of the Pentagon Papers, reaffirming that prior restraints come to the Court with a strong presumption against their constitutionality.

questions.prompt: Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) is most closely associated with which constitutional provision?
choices: The Establishment Clause, because the case banned religious instruction in public schools
The Free Exercise Clause, because the case excused Amish families from a compulsory school attendance law on religious grounds
The Equal Protection Clause, because the case prohibited segregated parochial schools
The Due Process Clause, because the case required notice before removing a child from school
questions.correctText: The Free Exercise Clause, because the case excused Amish families from a compulsory school attendance law on religious grounds
questions.explanation: The Court held that Wisconsin's compulsory school attendance law violated the Amish families' free exercise of religion by forcing them to send their children to school past 8th grade against their faith.

questions.prompt: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) is constitutionally significant because the Supreme Court held that
choices: separate but equal had no place in public education and that segregated schools violate the Equal Protection Clause
racially restrictive housing covenants could not be enforced in state courts
Congress could prohibit racial discrimination in private restaurants under the Commerce Clause
literacy tests for voting were prohibited regardless of how they were administered
questions.correctText: separate but equal had no place in public education and that segregated schools violate the Equal Protection Clause
questions.explanation: Brown overturned Plessy v. Ferguson's separate-but-equal doctrine in public schooling, finding that segregation was inherently unequal and violated the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause.

questions.prompt: In Letter from Birmingham Jail, Martin Luther King Jr. justifies civil disobedience by arguing that
choices: one has a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws openly, lovingly, and willingly accepting the penalty
any law passed by a majority is legitimate and must be obeyed regardless of its content
violent resistance is preferable to nonviolent protest when peaceful negotiations fail
civil rights activists should defer to state legislatures rather than petition the federal courts
questions.correctText: one has a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws openly, lovingly, and willingly accepting the penalty
questions.explanation: King distinguishes just from unjust laws and argues that breaking an unjust law openly and accepting punishment expresses the highest respect for law — a core defense of nonviolent direct action.

questions.prompt: McDonald v. Chicago (2010) is constitutionally significant because the Supreme Court
choices: incorporated the Second Amendment's right to keep and bear arms against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment
held that the Second Amendment protects only members of a well-regulated militia
permitted Chicago to enforce a city-wide handgun ban as a reasonable regulation
extended the Fourth Amendment's protections to private security guards
questions.correctText: incorporated the Second Amendment's right to keep and bear arms against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment
questions.explanation: McDonald used selective incorporation under the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause to apply the individual-rights reading of the Second Amendment (from Heller) to the states and cities.

questions.prompt: The selective incorporation doctrine refers to the process by which
choices: Congress passes statutes that override state laws when the two conflict
the Supreme Court applies specific protections of the Bill of Rights to the states case by case through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause
state legislatures may opt into federal Bill of Rights protections by passing identical statutes
federal agencies adopt the policies of cooperating states into their own regulations
questions.correctText: the Supreme Court applies specific protections of the Bill of Rights to the states case by case through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause
questions.explanation: Selective incorporation is the judicial process — running from Gitlow (1925) through Timbs (2019) — of recognizing individual Bill of Rights provisions as applicable to the states one at a time.

questions.prompt: The single most important agent of political socialization for most Americans is generally considered to be
choices: the family
social media platforms
membership in interest groups
religious institutions
questions.correctText: the family
questions.explanation: Decades of survey research show family is the strongest predictor of early party identification and basic political orientations. School, peers, media, and religion layer on top of that foundation.

questions.prompt: A telephone poll surveys 1,000 likely voters and reports 52% support for Candidate A with a margin of error of plus or minus 3 percentage points at a 95% confidence level. Which interpretation is most accurate?
choices: Candidate A is certain to receive between 49% and 55% of the vote on election day
Within the assumed sampling framework, the true level of support among likely voters is most plausibly between 49% and 55%
If Candidate A's true support is below 50%, the poll must have been intentionally biased
Repeating the poll would always yield exactly the same result
questions.correctText: Within the assumed sampling framework, the true level of support among likely voters is most plausibly between 49% and 55%
questions.explanation: Margins of error describe sampling uncertainty, not certainty about future behavior. With 52% and a 3-point margin the confidence interval straddles 50%, so the race is statistically too close to call from this poll alone.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements best characterizes the differences between modern American liberals and conservatives on economic policy?
choices: Liberals generally favor a larger government role in regulating the economy and providing a social safety net, while conservatives generally favor lower taxes, less regulation, and a smaller federal role
Liberals oppose all forms of taxation, while conservatives support universal government health insurance
Both groups favor identical economic policies but differ only on social issues
Conservatives generally favor expanding federal entitlement spending, while liberals oppose it
questions.correctText: Liberals generally favor a larger government role in regulating the economy and providing a social safety net, while conservatives generally favor lower taxes, less regulation, and a smaller federal role
questions.explanation: On economic questions, the liberal–conservative divide tracks the size and scope of government: liberals tend to back redistributive spending and regulation; conservatives tend to back lower taxes and a leaner federal hand.

questions.prompt: Which polling method is most likely to produce a representative sample of the American public?
choices: An online opt-in survey posted on a single news website's homepage
A random-digit-dial telephone survey supplemented with cellphone and online panels using weighting
Interviews conducted with attendees at a single political party's national convention
Survey responses solicited only from registered members of one political party
questions.correctText: A random-digit-dial telephone survey supplemented with cellphone and online panels using weighting
questions.explanation: Probability-based samples (random selection plus statistical weighting) generalize to the population. Opt-in samples and convenience samples introduce systematic selection bias.

questions.prompt: Suppose a quantitative study reports that voter turnout among adults with a college degree is roughly 75% in a recent presidential election, compared with about 50% among adults with only a high school diploma. Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Educational attainment is one of the strongest demographic predictors of political participation in the United States
All college-educated adults vote in every election, while no high-school-only adults vote
Education has no measurable relationship to political participation
Voter ID laws fully explain the gap in turnout between education groups
questions.correctText: Educational attainment is one of the strongest demographic predictors of political participation in the United States
questions.explanation: A 25-point gap is large and consistent with decades of turnout research showing education is among the most powerful demographic predictors of voting, alongside age and income.

questions.prompt: Keynesian and supply-side economic theories differ primarily in their views about
choices: whether the United States should remain on the gold standard
whether government should stimulate demand through spending and progressive taxation (Keynesian) or stimulate supply through tax cuts and deregulation (supply-side)
whether the Federal Reserve should be abolished
whether the federal budget must balance every fiscal year regardless of conditions
questions.correctText: whether government should stimulate demand through spending and progressive taxation (Keynesian) or stimulate supply through tax cuts and deregulation (supply-side)
questions.explanation: Keynesians emphasize managing aggregate demand through fiscal policy; supply-siders argue that lower marginal tax rates and lighter regulation expand productive capacity. The debate maps onto modern liberal–conservative economic disagreements.

questions.prompt: The libertarian ideology in American politics is best characterized by support for
choices: expansive federal regulation of both economic and social behavior
minimal government intervention in both economic markets and personal lifestyle decisions
a single-party state with little role for elections
national ownership of major industries and central economic planning
questions.correctText: minimal government intervention in both economic markets and personal lifestyle decisions
questions.explanation: Libertarians combine economic conservatism (low taxes, light regulation) with social liberalism (broad personal autonomy), distinguishing them from both major-party coalitions on key issues.

questions.prompt: The generational replacement and period effects observed in survey data suggest that
choices: political opinions are entirely fixed at birth and never change across the lifespan
cohorts socialized during distinctive historical periods often carry differing political attitudes throughout their adult lives, gradually shifting national opinion as cohorts turn over
voter turnout is identical across all generational cohorts in every election
young voters always hold the same opinions as their parents do
questions.correctText: cohorts socialized during distinctive historical periods often carry differing political attitudes throughout their adult lives, gradually shifting national opinion as cohorts turn over
questions.explanation: Public opinion shifts partly because distinctive cohorts (Depression-era, Boomers, Millennials, etc.) carry the imprint of the political environment in which they came of age.

questions.prompt: Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) is constitutionally significant because the Supreme Court held that
choices: Congress may impose any limits on campaign expenditures so long as they are content-neutral
the First Amendment prohibits the government from restricting independent political expenditures by corporations and unions
individual contributions to candidates are unlimited under the First Amendment
political parties may coordinate unlimited spending directly with candidates
questions.correctText: the First Amendment prohibits the government from restricting independent political expenditures by corporations and unions
questions.explanation: Citizens United struck down limits on independent political spending by corporations and unions, treating such expenditures as protected speech and giving rise to super PACs that may raise and spend unlimited sums independently of candidates.

questions.prompt: Shaw v. Reno (1993) is best understood as a case about
choices: racial gerrymandering, in which the Court held that bizarrely shaped majority-minority districts drawn predominantly on the basis of race are subject to strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause
partisan gerrymandering, in which the Court announced a clear constitutional standard for judging the fairness of district maps
one-person, one-vote, in which the Court required congressional districts to be of roughly equal population
voter identification, in which the Court upheld photo ID requirements
questions.correctText: racial gerrymandering, in which the Court held that bizarrely shaped majority-minority districts drawn predominantly on the basis of race are subject to strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause
questions.explanation: Shaw v. Reno held that when race is the predominant factor in drawing a district, strict scrutiny applies even if the goal is to increase minority representation.

questions.prompt: Baker v. Carr (1962) is constitutionally significant because the Supreme Court held that
choices: questions about legislative district apportionment are justiciable under the Equal Protection Clause and not purely political questions
states may draw legislative districts of dramatically unequal population so long as boundaries follow county lines
federal courts may not hear redistricting cases under any circumstances
the Voting Rights Act of 1965 was unconstitutional in its preclearance provisions
questions.correctText: questions about legislative district apportionment are justiciable under the Equal Protection Clause and not purely political questions
questions.explanation: Baker opened the federal courthouse door to apportionment challenges, paving the way for the 'one person, one vote' principle articulated in subsequent cases such as Reynolds v. Sims.

questions.prompt: Which of the following constitutional amendments most directly expanded the American electorate?
choices: The Tenth Amendment, by reserving powers to the states
The Nineteenth Amendment, by prohibiting denial of the right to vote on account of sex
The Twenty-Second Amendment, by limiting the president to two terms
The Twenty-Seventh Amendment, by regulating congressional pay raises
questions.correctText: The Nineteenth Amendment, by prohibiting denial of the right to vote on account of sex
questions.explanation: Among voting-rights amendments — the 15th, 19th, 24th, and 26th — only the 19th appears among the listed options. It enfranchised women nationwide in 1920.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the function of a political action committee (PAC) under federal campaign finance law?
choices: A government agency that administers federal election law and certifies vote totals
An organization that pools contributions from members and donates to candidates and parties within federal contribution limits
A judicial body that resolves disputes among congressional candidates
A branch of the executive office that supervises presidential campaigns
questions.correctText: An organization that pools contributions from members and donates to candidates and parties within federal contribution limits
questions.explanation: PACs are private organizations that collect contributions from members and channel them to candidates and parties, subject to FEC contribution limits. Super PACs are a distinct category that may raise unlimited sums but only for independent expenditures.

questions.prompt: Linkage institutions in American politics include all of the following EXCEPT
choices: political parties
interest groups
the news media
the federal judiciary
questions.correctText: the federal judiciary
questions.explanation: Linkage institutions — parties, interest groups, media, and elections — are the channels that connect citizens to government. The federal judiciary is a branch of government, not a linkage institution.

questions.prompt: The Electoral College and a direct national popular vote differ in that
choices: the Electoral College distributes electors mostly by population (with two extra per state), and the winner-take-all systems used by most states can produce a president who lost the national popular vote — outcomes a direct popular vote, by design, would not produce
the Electoral College guarantees that the winner of the national popular vote always becomes president
a direct popular vote would require an amendment to the Articles of Confederation
the Electoral College has never produced a president who lost the national popular vote
questions.correctText: the Electoral College distributes electors mostly by population (with two extra per state), and the winner-take-all systems used by most states can produce a president who lost the national popular vote — outcomes a direct popular vote, by design, would not produce
questions.explanation: Electoral votes equal each state's congressional delegation, and almost all states award their electors winner-take-all. Combined, those features can yield an Electoral College winner who finishes second in the national popular vote — most recently in 2000 and 2016.
